input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet needs to be run from one framework but it will manage running on both. Let me use `--framework net10.0` (the host) and BenchmarkDotNet will handle multi-targeting via its `[SimpleJob]` attributes or the `--runtimes` CLI flag.

Actually, with BenchmarkDotNet, the way to benchmark across multiple runtimes is to either use `[SimpleJob]` attributes specifying the runtimes, or pass `--runtimes` on the command line. Let me use the `--runtimes` CLI argument.

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet doesn't know `net10.0` yet. Let me use job attributes instead.

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-b5dde03b296349f1a597467e1ae36d5e/Benchmarks && dotnet run -c Release --framework net10.0 -- --runtimes net90 --join 2>&1 | head -5

data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.x doesn't have a `net10.0` runtime moniker yet. I'll use `[SimpleJob]` attributes for net9.0, and for net10.0 I'll need a different approach. Actually, the simplest approach is to use the `--runtimes net90` moniker for .NET 9 and then just run directly on net10.0 as the host process. But there's no `net100` moniker.

Let me think about this differently. I can:
1. Use `[SimpleJob(RuntimeMoniker.Net90)]` for .NET 9


2. For .NET 10, I'll need to work around the missing moniker by running the benchmark twice with different framework targets instead of relying on runtime attributes.

Actually, I'm realizing the `--runtimes` flag controls which runtime BDN targets, so if I specify `net90`, it'll only run against .NET 9. The simplest approach is just running the host process directly to compare both versions. of two separate runs. I could use Job attributes in the code instead — applying `[SimpleJob(RuntimeMoniker.Net90)]` for .NET 9.0 and checking what moniker exists for .NET 10, whether that's `Net100` or `HostProcess`. Let me see what's available in the current BDN version.
